10.1016/j.jmp.2020.102427
A first pass at developing a mathematical theory for knowledge structures was made by  Doignon and Falmagne (1985) . At the inception, the intention of knowledge structure theory was to offer a sound combinatoric framework to design an efficient machine for the assessment of knowledge (see  Doignon & Falmagne, 1999; Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). In this context, mastery with regard to a specific field of knowledge is tested by a collection of question types, or “items”. The field of knowledge, or “domain”, is simply identified with the full collection of items. A student’s mastery, or “knowledge state”, is the subset of items that she can answer correctly. The family of feasible knowledge states defines the “knowledge structure”. Short introductions to knowledge structure theory are contained in  Doignon and Falmagne (1987)  and  Falmagne (1989) . Some more leisurely paced texts are  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  and  Falmagne, Albert, Doble, Eppstein, and Hu (2013) .

Definition 1 
                         
                            Doignon & Falmagne, 1985 
                         
                         A knowledge structure is a pair  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             in which  
                               Q 
                             is a nonempty set, and  
                               K 
                             is a family of subsets of  
                               Q 
                            , containing at least  
                               Q 
                             and the empty set  
                               0̸ 
                            . The set  
                               Q 
                             is called the domain of the knowledge structure. Its elements are referred to as questions or items and the subsets in the family  
                               K 
                             are labeled (knowledge) states. In addition,  
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                               
                             denotes the subfamily  
                               
                                  { 
                                  K 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                                  
                                  | 
                                  
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                                  } 
                               
                             of  
                               K 
                             for  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                            . Further, a knowledge structure  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is a knowledge space (resp. simple closure space) if  
                               K 
                             is closed under union (resp. intersection).

A knowledge structure is a pair  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             in which  
                               Q 
                             is a nonempty set, and  
                               K 
                             is a family of subsets of  
                               Q 
                            , containing at least  
                               Q 
                             and the empty set  
                               0̸ 
                            . The set  
                               Q 
                             is called the domain of the knowledge structure. Its elements are referred to as questions or items and the subsets in the family  
                               K 
                             are labeled (knowledge) states. In addition,  
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                               
                             denotes the subfamily  
                               
                                  { 
                                  K 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                                  
                                  | 
                                  
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                                  } 
                               
                             of  
                               K 
                             for  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                            . Further, a knowledge structure  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is a knowledge space (resp. simple closure space) if  
                               K 
                             is closed under union (resp. intersection).

As a basic concept of knowledge structures, the separability of items plays an important role in knowledge structure theory (cf.  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). It is known that a discriminative knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       can always be manufactured from any knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       by forming the notions, where the knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       is called the discriminative reduction of  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       (cf.  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). Nevertheless, we are more interested in seeking characterizations of the separability of items in knowledge structures. Note that basic concepts of knowledge structure are consistent with traditional explanatory features of psychometric theory, such as “skill” (cf.  Lord & Novick, 1974; Wainer et al., 2000; Wainer & Messick, 1983; Weiss, 1983 ). Many of the details about skill maps were provided by  Doignon (1994) . Further results on skill multimaps can also be found in  Düntsch and Gediga (1995), Falmagne and Doignon (2011), Heller, Anselmi, Stefanutti, and Robusto (2017), Heller, Augustin, Hockemeyer, Stefanutti, and Albert (2013), Heller, Ünlü, and Albert (2013), Spoto, Stefanutti, and Vidotto (2012, 2013)  and  Stefanutti and de Chiusole (2017) . As an exercise in their book (Problem 9 in Chapter 6 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ), Falmagne and Doignon asked readers to find a necessary and sufficient condition on a disjunctive skill multimap ensuring that the delineated knowledge structure is discriminative.

It leads us to consider a more general question: what are the relationships between skill multimaps and the separability of items in delineated knowledge structures? That is, the following question is posed.

Question 1 
                         Suppose  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is the knowledge structure delineated by the skill multimap  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . Can we find some necessary and sufficient conditions, expressed in terms of competencies of  
                               μ 
                            , ensuring that  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is discriminative?

Suppose  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is the knowledge structure delineated by the skill multimap  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . Can we find some necessary and sufficient conditions, expressed in terms of competencies of  
                               μ 
                            , ensuring that  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is discriminative?

In this paper, we introduce the bi-separability as a bidirectional separability to deepen the separability of items in knowledge structures. Thus, the following question arises by replacing discriminative in  Question 1  with bi-discriminative.

Question 1* 
                         Suppose  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is the knowledge structure delineated by the skill multimap  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . Can we find some necessary and sufficient conditions, expressed in terms of competencies of  
                               μ 
                            , ensuring that  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is bi-discriminative?

Suppose  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is the knowledge structure delineated by the skill multimap  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . Can we find some necessary and sufficient conditions, expressed in terms of competencies of  
                               μ 
                            , ensuring that  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is bi-discriminative?

Our discussion spreads around  Questions 1  and  1* . We discuss the separability (resp. the bi-separability) of items in the delineated knowledge structure. With the help of separations in topology, we obtain some necessary and sufficient conditions for a knowledge structure delineated by a skill multimap, expressed in terms of competencies of the skill multimap, such that the delineated knowledge structure is discriminative (resp. bi-discriminative), which gives an answer to  Question 1 . As applications of these results, the separability and the bi-separability are discussed around distributed skill functions and the meshing of the delineated knowledge structures.

Let  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       be a knowledge structure, and let  
                         
                            q 
                            , 
                            r 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                      . We say that  
                         
                            q 
                            , 
                            r 
                         
                       carry the same information relative to  
                         K 
                       if they are contained in the same states, i.e., any state containing  
                         q 
                       also contains  
                         r 
                      , and vice versa. In other terms, we have  
                         
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  r 
                               
                            
                         
                      . From a practical viewpoint, it implies that any individual whose state contains item  
                         q 
                       has necessarily mastered item  
                         r 
                      , and vice versa ( Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). On the other hand, we say an item is not a prerequisite of another item if there is a state containing the latter does not contain the former. Then the separability of  
                         q 
                       and  
                         r 
                       means that  
                         r 
                       is not a prerequisite of  
                         q 
                       or  
                         q 
                       is not a prerequisite of  
                         r 
                      . Consider the bidirectional separability (abbr. bi-separability) of  
                         q 
                       and  
                         r 
                      :  
                         r 
                       is not a prerequisite of  
                         q 
                       and  
                         q 
                       is not a prerequisite of  
                         r 
                      , i.e., there is not any prerequisite relation between  
                         q 
                       and  
                         r 
                      . In such a knowledge structure, every item is “independent” from the other items and we call it bi-discriminative. In the following, the definition of discriminative (resp. bi-discriminative) knowledge structure is given. In order to introduce bi-discriminative knowledge structure more naturally, we introduce discriminative knowledge structures by the description given in  Doignon, Falmagne, and Cosyn (2013) .

Definition 2

From  Definition 2 , we can see that bi-discriminative knowledge structures are bidirectionally discriminative. It is clear that bi-discriminative  
                         ⟹ 
                       discriminative. However, the converse implication is invalid (see  Example 2 ).

Remark 1 
                         Let  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             be a knowledge structure. 
                         
                            
                               (1) 
                             For  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                            , put  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     r 
                                     ∈ 
                                     Q 
                                     
                                     | 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           K 
                                        
                                        
                                           q 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           K 
                                        
                                        
                                           r 
                                        
                                     
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            . Then  
                               
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                             is called the notion of  
                               q 
                             in  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . Equivalently, the separability of items in  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             can be also defined by the notion:  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is called discriminative if  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     q 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                             for each  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                             (see  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). 
                         
                            
                               (2) 
                             For two items  
                               
                                  q 
                                  , 
                                  r 
                               
                             in  
                               Q 
                            ,  
                               q 
                             is called a prerequisite of  
                               r 
                             and write  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ≾ 
                                  r 
                               
                             if  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  ⋂ 
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        r 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            . The prerequisite relation  
                               ≾ 
                             is also called the precedence relation on the knowledge structure  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             (see, e.g.  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). Thus,  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is discriminative (resp. bi-discriminative) if and only if  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ⪯̸ 
                                  r 
                               
                             or  
                               
                                  r 
                                  ⪯̸ 
                                  q 
                               
                             (resp.  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ⪯̸ 
                                  r 
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  r 
                                  ⪯̸ 
                                  q 
                               
                            ) for all  
                               
                                  q 
                                  , 
                                  r 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                             with  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ≠ 
                                  r 
                               
                            .

( 
                            U

                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            ,

The knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  L 
                               
                               
                                  ′

                       in  Example 2  (i.e., Example 2.2.8 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ) and the knowledge structure

                            G 
                            ) 
                         
                       in Example 2.4.7 of  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  are bi-discriminative, which also shows that bi-discriminative knowledge structures appear in our discussion. To further demonstrate the usefulness of bi-discriminative knowledge structures, we consider a class exercise about quadratic radical. As a mathematical psychology model of this class exercise, a bi-discriminative knowledge structure is constructed in the following example.

It is not difficult to check that  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is bi-discriminative. So there is not any prerequisite relation among  
                               
                                  q 
                                  , 
                                  r 
                                  , 
                                  t 
                               
                             from  Remark 1 (2). It shows that each item in  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is “independent” from the other items in this class exercise. It is helpful for us to view the mastery of knowledge for students about quadratic radical from another perspective.

Similar to the class exercise in  Example 3 , such cases often appear in knowledge assessment, where the corresponding knowledge structures are generally bi-discriminative. Furthermore, we are interesting in bi-discriminative knowledge structures in two aspects. On one hand, there is a difficulty to knowledge assessment in real-life cases. This difficulty is namely that human behavior in testing situations is often unreliable: subjects may temporarily forget the solution of a problem that they know well, or make careless errors in responding (see Page 153 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). On the other hand, sometimes an item can be mastered via one or more learning tracks in knowledge learning (see Page 96 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). These features show that the existing ways are inadequate as knowledge assessment devices, which leads us to consider further enriching and deepening the methods of knowledge assessment. As an application of bi-discriminative knowledge structures in knowledge assessment, it may be useful to apply the “relative independence” of items in bi-discriminative knowledge structures. This makes it possible that the bi-separability of items in knowledge structures could be employed as a tool to separate the assessment questions.

In order to characterize the separability and the bi-separability of items in knowledge structures, some separations in knowledge structures are introduced as in topological spaces (e.g. see  Engelking, 1989 ).

Definition 3 
                         Let  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             be a knowledge structure. 
                         
                            
                               (1)

Note that a knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a topological space if  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a knowledge space and  
                         K 
                       is closed under finite intersection. Similar to  Definition 3 ,  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                      -topological space (resp.  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                      -topological space) can be defined (e.g. see  Engelking, 1989 ). It is known that every topological space is a knowledge structure and the reverse is not true. However, a knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       can generate a topological space  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            T 
                            ) 
                         
                       such that  
                         K 
                       is a subbase of the topology  
                         T 
                      . That is,  
                         T 
                       consists of all unions of elements of  
                         ℬ 
                      , where  
                         ℬ 
                       consists of all intersections of finitely many elements of  
                         K 
                      . It is not difficult to prove that  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                      -knowledge structure (resp.  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                      -knowledge structure) if and only if  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            T 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                      -topological space (resp.  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                      -topological space).

The following lemma shows the equivalence between discriminative knowledge structures (resp. bi-discriminative knowledge structures) and  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                      -knowledge structures (resp.  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                      -knowledge structures). The proof is simple and we omit it.

                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     0

                                  Q

                                  K 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            
                            -knowledge structure.

By  Lemma 1 , we give a simple variant of a theorem in media theory to demonstrate briefly the relevance of the bi-separability in media theory. The following theory was given by  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  as an interesting result of media theory. Due to space limitations, we will not explain the concepts involved in this theorem here. One can refer to  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  for more details.

In the above discussion on knowledge structures, knowledge is represented by the set-theoretic approach, which uses the design of automatic procedures for the assessment of knowledge (e.g.  Doignon & Falmagne ,  1985, 1988a, 1988b ). Following another approach (see, e.g.  Marshall, 1981 ), knowledge is explained by skills: a student masters all the questions for which he or she possesses the required skills. From  Marshall (1981)  and others ( Albert, Schrepp, & Held, 1992; Falmagne, Koppen, Villano, Doignon, & Johannesen, 1990; Lukas & Albert, 1993 ), we assume the existence of some basic set  
                         S 
                       of “skills”. These skills may consist in methods, algorithms or tricks which could in principle be identified. The idea is to associate with each question  
                         q 
                       in the domain, the skills in  
                         S 
                       which are useful or instrumental to solve this question, and to deduce from this association what the knowledge states are (see Page 103 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). This linkage between the skills and questions can be formalized by a skill map.

Definition 5 
                         
                            Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 
                         
                         A skill map is a triple  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  τ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            , where  
                               Q 
                             is a nonempty set of items,  
                               S 
                             is a nonempty set of skills, and  
                               τ 
                             is a mapping from  
                               Q 
                             to  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                     
                                        S 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∖ 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     0̸ 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            .

A skill map is a triple  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  τ

                            , where

                                  ∖

                            .

In the link between skill maps and knowledge structures, there exist two important models: the disjunctive model and the conjunctive model.

From a practical viewpoint, the disjunctive model assumes that a question is mastered as soon as at least one of its assigned skills is acquired by the student; the conjunctive model, on the contrary, states that all of the assigned skills are necessary for the mastery of a question ( Doignon, 1994 ). For the disjunctive model and conjunctive model, the following proposition is known.

Proposition 2 
                         
                            Doignon, 1994 
                         
                         
                            (1) Any knowledge structure delineated via the disjunctive model by a skill map is a knowledge space. Conversely, any knowledge space is delineated via the disjunctive model by at least one skill map. 
                         
                         
                            (2) The knowledge structures delineated via the disjunctive and conjunctive model by the same skill map are dual one to the other. As a consequence, the knowledge structures delineated via the conjunctive model are exactly the simple closure spaces.

(1) Any knowledge structure delineated via the disjunctive model by a skill map is a knowledge space. Conversely, any knowledge space is delineated via the disjunctive model by at least one skill map.

(2) The knowledge structures delineated via the disjunctive and conjunctive model by the same skill map are dual one to the other. As a consequence, the knowledge structures delineated via the conjunctive model are exactly the simple closure spaces.

The delineated knowledge structure via disjunctive or conjunctive model deals with skill maps that are set-valued maps. In the following sections, we will deal with the generalization of skill map: skill multimap.

The main idea of the skill multimap is as follows: Identifying the skills that are sufficient for solving each of the considered items provides a complete characterization of the solution behavior. The set of items which can be solved within a given set of skills is uniquely defined. This subset of items constitutes a possible knowledge state, and their collection forms a knowledge structure (see  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). Let the nonempty set  
                         Q 
                       be a knowledge domain and let  
                         S 
                       be a nonempty set of abstract skills that are assumed to be relevant for solving the items in  
                         Q 
                      . The case that the basic sets  
                         Q 
                       and  
                         S 
                       are infinite was discussed by  Doignon (1994)  and  Falmagne and Doignon (2011) . The case that the basic sets  
                         Q 
                       and  
                         S 
                       are finite was treated by  Düntsch and Gediga (1995)  and  Gediga and Düntsch (2002)  as well as by  Korossy (1997, 1999) . In our work, the basic sets  
                         Q 
                       and  
                         S 
                       may be finite or infinite. In the following definition, we refer to the terminology of  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  and  Heller, Augustin, Hockemeyer, Stefanutti, and Albert (2013), Heller, Ünlü, and Albert (2013) , which slightly differs from that introduced by  Doignon (1994) .

Definition 7 
                         
                            Falmagne & Doignon, 2011; Heller, Augustin, Hockemeyer, Stefanutti, & Albert, 2013; Heller, Ünlü, & Albert, 2013 
                         
                         A skill multimap is a triple  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            , where  
                               Q 
                             is a nonempty set of items,  
                               S 
                             is a nonempty set of skills, and  
                               
                                  μ 
                                  : 
                                  Q 
                                  ⟶ 
                                  
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                           
                                              S 
                                           
                                        
                                        ∖ 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           0̸ 
                                           } 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  ∖ 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     0̸ 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                             is a mapping. For each  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                            , the elements of  
                               
                                  μ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             are called competencies. Additionally, if the competencies of  
                               
                                  μ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             are pairwise incomparable (with respect to set inclusion), then  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is called a skill function. Whenever the basic sets  
                               Q 
                             and  
                               S 
                             are clear from the context, we simply use  
                               μ 
                             to refer to the respective skill multimap, or skill function.

A skill multimap is a triple  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            , where  
                               Q 
                             is a nonempty set of items,  
                               S 
                             is a nonempty set of skills, and  
                               
                                  μ 
                                  : 
                                  Q 
                                  ⟶ 
                                  
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                           
                                              S 
                                           
                                        
                                        ∖ 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           0̸ 
                                           } 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  ∖ 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     0̸ 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                             is a mapping. For each  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                            , the elements of  
                               
                                  μ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             are called competencies. Additionally, if the competencies of  
                               
                                  μ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             are pairwise incomparable (with respect to set inclusion), then  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is called a skill function. Whenever the basic sets  
                               Q 
                             and  
                               S 
                             are clear from the context, we simply use  
                               μ 
                             to refer to the respective skill multimap, or skill function.

A skill multimap, or a skill function may assign more than one competency to an item, representing the fact that there may be more than one set of cognitive operations for solving it. These alternatives can, for example, correspond to different solution paths that may be observed. The skills contained in any of the competencies of a skill multimap are assumed to be sufficient for solving the item. Introducing the notion of a skill function is motivated by the idea that the assigned competencies should even be minimally sufficient for solving the item. This means that any proper subset of skills is no longer sufficient for the solution. Minimality in this sense suggests the assumption that competencies assigned to an item are pairwise incomparable, a property which subsequently will be called incomparability condition ( Heller, Ünlü, & Albert, 2013 ).

Remark 3 
                         Recall that a skill multimap  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is disjunctive if for all  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                             the competencies  
                               
                                  C 
                                  ∈ 
                                  μ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             are singletons;  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is conjunctive if for all  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  μ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     C 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                             for some  
                               
                                  C 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  S 
                               
                            . It is clear that both disjunctive skill multimaps and conjunctive skill multimaps are skill functions. It is known that a knowledge structure delineated by a disjunctive skill multimap is a knowledge space. In general, a delineated knowledge structure need not be a knowledge space. It is natural to ask which kind of skill multimaps delineate knowledge spaces. This question is still open and some partial answers were obtained (e.g. see  Heller, Ünlü, & Albert, 2013 ).

Recall that a skill multimap  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is disjunctive if for all  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                             the competencies  
                               
                                  C 
                                  ∈ 
                                  μ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             are singletons;  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is conjunctive if for all  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  μ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     q 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     C 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                             for some  
                               
                                  C 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  S 
                               
                            . It is clear that both disjunctive skill multimaps and conjunctive skill multimaps are skill functions. It is known that a knowledge structure delineated by a disjunctive skill multimap is a knowledge space. In general, a delineated knowledge structure need not be a knowledge space. It is natural to ask which kind of skill multimaps delineate knowledge spaces. This question is still open and some partial answers were obtained (e.g. see  Heller, Ünlü, & Albert, 2013 ).

The delineation of arbitrary knowledge structures will be achieved by generalizing the concept of skill map. The intuition behind this generalization is natural enough. To each item  
                         q 
                      , we associate a collection  
                         
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       of subsets of skills. Any subset  
                         C 
                       of skills in  
                         
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       can be viewed as a method—called “competency” in Definition 7—for solving question  
                         q 
                      . Thus, possessing just one of these competencies is sufficient to solve question  
                         q 
                       (see Page 122 of  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ).

Now, with the help of separation, we give some necessary and sufficient conditions, expressed in terms of skill multimaps, ensuring that the delineated knowledge structures are discriminative or bi-discriminative. To this end, we introduce the concept of refining a family of subsets.

                               V

                                  V

                                  V

                                  U

                                  V

                               U

                               V

                                  U

                                  ⋐ 
                                  V 
                               
                            . In particular, if  
                               
                                  U

                                     }

                               U 
                             is (resp. is not) refined by  
                               V

                                  U 
                                  ⋐ 
                                  V 
                               
                             (resp.  
                               
                                  U 
                                  ⁄ 
                                  ⋐ 
                                  V 
                               
                            ).

By  Remark 3 , we give the following consequence as a direct result of  Theorem 1 .

Corollary 1 
                         
                            A disjunctive skill multimap (resp. a conjunctive skill multimap) is injective if and only if the delineated knowledge structure is discriminative. 
                         
                         
                            As a further consideration of  
                            
                               Theorem  1 
                            , the following example shows that the assumption “ 
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a skill function” in  
                            
                               Theorem  1  
                            (3)  
                            
                               ⟹ 
                             
                            (1) cannot be omitted.

A disjunctive skill multimap (resp. a conjunctive skill multimap) is injective if and only if the delineated knowledge structure is discriminative.

As a further consideration of  
                            
                               Theorem  1 
                            , the following example shows that the assumption “ 
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  ,

                            is a skill function” in

The following theorem is interesting and more directly related to traditional knowledge structure theory. Let  
                         U 
                       be a family consisting of subsets of a set  
                         Q

                                  = 
                                  p

                                     T

                            μ

                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            q 
                            , 
                            r 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                       with  
                         
                            q 
                            ≠ 
                            r 
                         
                      ” equivalent? The answer is “No”.
